journal_entry.rationale: Inventory is an asset account that increases when purchased. Cash is an asset that decreases when paid out. The debit tuple (1,0,0,0,0,0) indicates one asset increase (Inventory), and the credit tuple (0,0,0,1,0,0) indicates one asset decrease (Cash). This follows standard accounting treatment for inventory purchases under IFRS - inventory is capitalized as an asset until sold, at which point it becomes cost of goods sold.

output.reason: Inventory purchase = asset increase. Under IFRS, inventory is recorded as an asset when acquired and only becomes an expense (COGS) when sold.

debit_classifier.reason: Inventory purchase = asset increase. Under IFRS, inventory is recorded as an asset when acquired and only becomes an expense (COGS) when sold.

output.reason: Cash is paid out to purchase inventory. Cash is an asset, and when cash leaves the business, it represents an asset decrease. Inventory is also an asset, but that is on the debit side (asset increase). The credit side only reflects the cash payment.

credit_classifier.reason: Cash is paid out to purchase inventory. Cash is an asset, and when cash leaves the business, it represents an asset decrease. Inventory is also an asset, but that is on the debit side (asset increase). The credit side only reflects the cash payment.
